content.body: A force is a push or a pull on an object. Forces can make objects start moving, stop moving, speed up, slow down, or change direction. Forces can also change the shape of an object.
Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton.

There are many types of forces: Gravity pulls objects downward toward the Earth. Friction opposes motion between two surfaces. Normal force acts perpendicular to a surface. Applied force is a push or pull by a person or machine. Tension acts through ropes and strings.

content.title: Forces in Daily Life
content.body:
When you kick a football:
• Your foot applies a force on the ball (applied force)
• The ball moves in the direction of the force
• Eventually friction and air resistance slow it down
• Gravity pulls it back to the ground

Newton's Second Law: Force = Mass × Acceleration
F = ma
Where F is in Newtons (N), m is in kilograms (kg), a is in metres per second squared (m/s²)

content.title: Calculating Force
content.body:
A box of mass 5 kg accelerates at 3 m/s². What force acts on it?
F = ma = 5 × 3 = 15 N

questions.question: What is a force?
options: A push or pull on an object
questions.answer: A push or pull on an object
questions.explanation: A force is defined as a push or pull acting on an object.

questions.question: What unit is force measured in?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N), named after Sir Isaac Newton.

questions.question: A 10 kg object accelerates at 2 m/s². What force acts on it?
Which force always pulls objects toward the Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravity is the force that pulls all objects toward the centre of the Earth.

questions.question: What does friction do to moving objects?
questions.explanation: Friction is a force that opposes motion between two surfaces in contact.

questions.question: If F = 30 N and m = 6 kg, what is the acceleration?
What happens to an object when a net force acts on it?
questions.explanation: A net force causes an object to accelerate (change its velocity).

questions.question: Which force acts through a rope?
questions.explanation: Tension is the force transmitted through a rope, string or cable.

questions.question: What does Newton's Second Law state?
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: Force equals mass multiplied by acceleration (F = ma).

questions.question: A force of 24 N acts on a 8 kg mass. What is the acceleration?
What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: Speed is how fast an object moves — it tells us the distance covered per unit of time. Velocity is speed in a specific direction. Acceleration is the rate at which velocity changes.

Speed = Distance ÷ Time
v = d/t
Unit: metres per second (m/s)

Acceleration = Change in Velocity ÷ Time
a = (v − u) ÷ t
Where v = final velocity, u = initial velocity, t = time
Unit: m/s²

content.title: Calculating Speed
content.body:
A car travels 120 m in 8 seconds. What is its speed?
v = d/t = 120/8 = 15 m/s

content.title: Calculating Acceleration
content.body:
A motorbike goes from 0 m/s to 20 m/s in 5 seconds. What is its acceleration?
a = (v − u)/t = (20 − 0)/5 = 4 m/s²
Deceleration is negative acceleration — when an object slows down. If a car goes from 30 m/s to 10 m/s in 4 seconds: a = (10 − 30)/4 = −5 m/s² (the negative sign shows deceleration).

questions.question: What is the formula for speed?
questions.explanation: Speed = Distance ÷ Time (v = d/t)

questions.question: A student runs 200 m in 25 seconds. What is their speed?
What is the unit of acceleration?
questions.explanation: Acceleration is measured in metres per second squared (m/s²).

questions.question: A car accelerates from 10 m/s to 30 m/s in 5 s. What is its acceleration?
How is velocity different from speed?
questions.explanation: Velocity is speed in a specific direction. Speed has no direction.

questions.question: A bus travels at 20 m/s for 60 seconds. How far does it travel?
What does a negative acceleration mean?
questions.explanation: Negative acceleration means the object is slowing down (decelerating).

questions.question: A boda boda goes from 15 m/s to 0 m/s in 3 seconds. What is the deceleration?
questions.explanation: a = (0−15)/3 = −15/3 = −5 m/s² (negative = deceleration)

questions.question: If a car travels at 25 m/s, how long does it take to cover 500 m?
An object starts at rest and reaches 12 m/s in 4 seconds. What is its acceleration?
What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

content.body: Pressure is force per unit area. Moments measure the turning effect of a force.

Pressure:
P = F/A
P = pressure (Pa or N/m²), F = force (N), A = area (m²)

Higher pressure = same force on smaller area
Examples: sharp knife (small area → high pressure), snowshoes (large area → low pressure)

Moment of a force:
Moment = Force × perpendicular distance from pivot
Unit: N·m

Principle of moments (equilibrium):
Sum of clockwise moments = Sum of anticlockwise moments

content.title: Moments Example
content.body:
A 60 N force acts 2 m from a pivot.
Moment = 60 × 2 = 120 N·m clockwise

For balance: anticlockwise moment must also = 120 N·m
If the other force is 40 N, distance = 120/40 = 3 m

questions.question: Pressure formula is...
questions.explanation: Pressure = Force ÷ Area. Same force over smaller area gives higher pressure.

questions.question: A 200 N force on 0.5 m² area. Pressure =
Why do snowshoes prevent sinking in snow?
options: Large area reduces pressure on snow
questions.answer: Large area reduces pressure on snow
questions.explanation: Snowshoes spread body weight over large area → less pressure → don't sink into snow.

questions.question: Moment = Force × ...
questions.explanation: Moment of a force = Force × perpendicular distance from the pivot (fulcrum).

questions.question: A 30 N force 4 m from pivot. Moment =
questions.explanation: Moment = F × d = 30 × 4 = 120 N·m

questions.question: The principle of moments states...
questions.explanation: For equilibrium: sum of clockwise moments about any point = sum of anticlockwise moments.

questions.question: A seesaw: 40 N at 3 m left. What force at 2 m right balances it?
Pascal's unit of pressure (Pa) equals...
questions.explanation: 1 Pa = 1 N/m² — one newton per square metre.

questions.question: A needle has very small tip area. This...
options: Increases pressure making it penetrate easily
questions.answer: Increases pressure making it penetrate easily
questions.explanation: Tiny area → very high pressure for same force → needle penetrates materials easily.

questions.question: Hydraulic systems use pressure to...
questions.explanation: In hydraulic systems, pressure is transmitted through liquid. Small force on small piston creates large force on large piston.

content.body: Friction is a force that opposes relative motion between surfaces in contact. Gravity is the attractive force between masses. Weight is the gravitational force on an object.

Weight vs Mass:
Weight W = mg
W = weight (N), m = mass (kg), g = gravitational field strength (9.8 N/kg on Earth)

Mass = amount of matter (kg) — same everywhere
Weight = gravitational force (N) — varies with location

content.title: Terminal Velocity
content.body:
A falling object accelerates due to gravity. Air resistance increases with speed. Eventually air resistance = weight → no net force → constant terminal velocity.

Skydivers reach ~55 m/s terminal velocity. Opening parachute increases area → more air resistance → new lower terminal velocity (~5 m/s for safe landing)

Friction:
Static friction — prevents objects from moving
Kinetic friction — opposes sliding motion
f = μN (μ = coefficient of friction, N = normal force)

Friction is useful: walking, brakes, writing
Friction is wasteful: engine parts, causes heat

questions.question: Weight equals...
questions.explanation: W = mg where g = 9.8 N/kg on Earth's surface.

questions.question: A 5 kg object on Earth (g=10 N/kg). Weight =
On the Moon (g=1.6 N/kg), mass of an object...
questions.explanation: Mass is the amount of matter — it doesn't change with location. Only weight changes.

questions.question: Terminal velocity is reached when...
questions.explanation: Terminal velocity: air resistance = gravity, no net force, no acceleration → constant speed.

questions.question: Friction always acts...
questions.explanation: Friction opposes the relative motion or tendency of motion between surfaces.

questions.question: Which scenario uses friction beneficially?
questions.explanation: Brake pads use friction to slow rotation of wheels. Without friction, brakes wouldn't work.

questions.question: If weight = 98 N and g = 9.8 N/kg, mass =
Air resistance increases as speed...
questions.explanation: Air resistance (drag) is greater at higher speeds — faster objects push through more air per second.

questions.question: A skydiver opens parachute. What happens to terminal velocity?
questions.explanation: Parachute increases surface area → more air resistance → new equilibrium at lower speed.

questions.question: Static friction is...
options: The friction preventing stationary objects from moving
questions.answer: The friction preventing stationary objects from moving
questions.explanation: Static friction acts on objects at rest, preventing them from starting to move. It can be greater than kinetic friction.

questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Forces & Motion. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Forces & Motion concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Forces & Motion builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Forces & Motion. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Forces & Motion. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Forces & Motion include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Forces & Motion. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
